subject: Compliance Audits
title: Part 4 - Global Compliance Knowledge Map
description: A connected revision map for SOX, COBIT, PCI DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, and ISO/IEC 27001. It explains what each framework protects, who it applies to, how they relate, and how common controls support different compliance goals.

topics.title: Global Compliance Purpose
topics.definition: Compliance frameworks define obligations, governance structures, security requirements, and evidence expectations for different kinds of protected information and business risk.
keyPoints: The frameworks are not isolated topics.
Each framework protects a different business interest or data type.
SOX protects financial-reporting reliability.
COBIT organizes enterprise IT governance.
PCI DSS protects payment card data.
HIPAA protects health information.
GDPR protects personal data and individual privacy.
ISO/IEC 27001 provides an information security management system that can support many compliance obligations.
workflows: Compliance -> Protected interest -> Required controls -> Evidence -> Monitoring -> Improvement
oneLiners: Compliance becomes easier when each framework is mapped to what it protects.

topics.title: Current Compliance Memory Map
topics.definition: The compliance knowledge map connects legal requirements, governance frameworks, industry standards, and security management systems.
keyPoints: SOX belongs to financial reporting and internal controls.
COBIT belongs to IT governance, risk, controls, processes, and maturity.
PCI DSS belongs to payment security and cardholder data protection.
HIPAA belongs to healthcare privacy, security, and breach notification.
GDPR belongs to personal data protection and individual rights.
ISO 27001 belongs to security management and risk reduction.
oneLiners: Money, IT, cards, patients, personal data, and security management each have their own place.

topics.title: Framework Type Comparison
topics.definition: The frameworks differ by type, protected subject, and scope of application.
keyPoints: SOX is a United States federal law protecting financial reporting for public companies and applicable SEC-registered entities.
COBIT is a governance framework protecting enterprise IT governance and applicable to any enterprise that adopts it.
PCI DSS is an industry security standard protecting payment card data for merchants and payment service providers.
HIPAA is a United States federal law protecting protected health information for healthcare organizations and business associates.
GDPR is a privacy law protecting personal data of EU residents.
ISO 27001 is an international management-system standard for information security.
comparisons: PCI DSS = security standard for payment cards.
GDPR = privacy law for personal data.
oneLiners: Type matters: law, framework, standard, and management system are not the same.

topics.title: SOX Position in Compliance
topics.definition: SOX focuses on reliable financial reporting, internal controls, audit integrity, executive accountability, PCAOB oversight, and auditor independence.
keyPoints: SOX protects investors through reliable financial reporting.
It relies on internal controls and audit evidence.
CEO and CFO certification creates executive accountability.
PCAOB oversight strengthens audit quality.
IT becomes relevant when systems affect financial data or reporting.
workflows: Financial Statements -> Financial Reporting Systems -> Internal Controls -> Audit Evidence -> SOX Compliance
oneLiners: SOX is about trust in financial reporting.

topics.title: COBIT Position in Compliance
topics.definition: COBIT is an IT governance framework that helps organize controls, processes, responsibilities, risks, measurement, and maturity.
keyPoints: COBIT aligns IT with business objectives.
It supports governance, risk management, control objectives, process measurement, and continuous improvement.
COBIT can help implement IT controls that support SOX, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR.
COBIT is not a law; it is adopted as a framework.
workflows: Business Goals -> IT Goals -> IT Processes -> Controls -> Metrics -> Maturity Improvement
oneLiners: COBIT makes IT governance measurable.

topics.title: PCI DSS Position in Compliance
topics.definition: PCI DSS is an industry security standard focused on protecting cardholder data and payment systems.
keyPoints: PCI DSS applies to merchants and payment service providers handling payment card data.
It is maintained by the PCI Security Standards Council.
It is commonly associated with 12 major requirement areas.
Its control themes include firewalls, encryption, access control, monitoring, and security testing.
workflows: Sensitive Data -> Payment Card Data -> Cardholder Protection -> Firewalls + Encryption + Access Control + Monitoring + Security Testing
oneLiners: PCI DSS protects cards and payment environments.

topics.title: HIPAA Position in Compliance
topics.definition: HIPAA protects health information through privacy, security, and breach-notification requirements.
keyPoints: HIPAA focuses on Protected Health Information, often abbreviated as PHI.
It applies to healthcare providers, health plans, healthcare clearinghouses, and business associates.
It uses administrative, technical, and physical safeguards.
Its main concern is patient privacy and controlled use of health information.
workflows: Sensitive Data -> Health Information -> PHI -> Patient Privacy -> Administrative Safeguards + Technical Safeguards + Physical Safeguards
oneLiners: HIPAA protects patients through PHI safeguards.

topics.title: GDPR Position in Compliance
topics.definition: GDPR is a privacy law built on the idea that personal data belongs to the individual and organizations are custodians of that information.
keyPoints: GDPR protects personal data, privacy, and individual rights.
GDPR may apply based on the location of the individual whose data is processed, not only the location of the company.
It applies when organizations process data of EU residents, even if the organization is outside the European Union.
GDPR treats processing broadly: collection, storage, use, sharing, alteration, deletion, and destruction can all count.
workflows: Personal Data -> Individual Rights -> Lawful Processing -> Accountability -> Privacy Protection
oneLiners: GDPR shifts control of personal data back toward the individual.

topics.title: ISO 27001 Position in Compliance
topics.definition: ISO/IEC 27001 defines how an organization manages information security through an Information Security Management System.
keyPoints: ISO 27001 is not a privacy law.
It is a management-system standard for information security.
It treats security as an ongoing process involving people, processes, technology, risk, and continuous improvement.
ISO 27001 can support compliance with SOX, GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and other obligations, but certification alone does not automatically satisfy those laws or standards.
workflows: Security -> Management -> Risk Reduction -> Continuous Improvement -> ISMS
oneLiners: ISO 27001 manages security; it does not replace every regulation.

topics.title: SOX to COBIT Connection
topics.definition: SOX creates legal expectations for financial controls, while COBIT provides governance structure for designing and managing IT controls that support those expectations.
keyPoints: SOX says effective financial controls are required.
COBIT says how IT governance and controls can be built and improved.
COBIT helps translate SOX obligations into control objectives, owners, activities, evidence, testing, and monitoring.
workflows: SOX requirement -> Financial-control objective -> IT system dependency -> COBIT control objective -> IT control -> Evidence -> Assurance
oneLiners: SOX states the obligation; COBIT structures the control response.

topics.title: PCI DSS Control Flow
topics.definition: PCI DSS starts with sensitive payment card data and builds security around storage, transmission, access, monitoring, and testing.
keyPoints: Cardholder data must be protected wherever it is stored, processed, or transmitted.
Firewalls reduce unauthorized network exposure.
Encryption protects cardholder data in storage or transmission.
Access control limits who can reach payment systems.
Monitoring detects suspicious activity.
Security testing validates whether controls continue to work.
workflows: Payment Card Data -> Protect Cardholder Information -> Firewalls -> Encryption -> Access Control -> Monitoring -> Security Testing
oneLiners: PCI DSS follows the card data.

topics.title: HIPAA Safeguard Flow
topics.definition: HIPAA begins with health information and requires safeguards that protect patient privacy and security.
keyPoints: HIPAA protects PHI and electronic PHI.
Administrative safeguards include policies, workforce controls, training, and risk management.
Technical safeguards include access control, audit controls, integrity controls, and transmission security.
Physical safeguards protect facilities, devices, and media.
workflows: Health Information -> PHI -> Patient Privacy -> Administrative Safeguards -> Technical Safeguards -> Physical Safeguards
oneLiners: HIPAA safeguards health data through policy, technology, and physical protection.

topics.title: Separation by Data Type
topics.definition: A simple way to understand compliance is to map the protected data type to the relevant framework.
keyPoints: Financial statements map to SOX.
Enterprise IT maps to COBIT.
Credit card data maps to PCI DSS.
Patient health data maps to HIPAA.
Personal data maps to GDPR.
Information security management maps to ISO 27001.
oneLiners: Identify the data first, then identify the obligation.

topics.title: Unified Compliance Stack
topics.definition: An enterprise can organize compliance into governance, financial compliance, payment compliance, healthcare compliance, privacy compliance, and security management.
keyPoints: Governance is supported by COBIT.
Financial compliance is supported by SOX.
Payment compliance is supported by PCI DSS.
Healthcare compliance is supported by HIPAA.
Privacy compliance is supported by GDPR.
Security management is supported by ISO 27001.
oneLiners: Compliance is easier when obligations are grouped by business domain.

topics.title: Core Security Objectives
topics.definition: Each framework protects a different business objective, but all rely on security controls and governance evidence.
keyPoints: SOX protects financial reporting, internal controls, and audit integrity.
COBIT manages IT governance, risk, business alignment, and control objectives.
PCI DSS protects cardholder data, payment systems, and transaction security.
HIPAA protects PHI, patient privacy, and electronic health records.
GDPR protects personal data, privacy, lawful processing, and individual rights.
ISO 27001 protects confidentiality, integrity, and availability through an ISMS.
oneLiners: The control may look similar, but the compliance objective changes.

topics.title: Common Controls Across Frameworks
topics.definition: Different frameworks often rely on similar security controls, even when they protect different data types.
keyPoints: Common controls include identity management, least privilege, authentication, logging, monitoring, encryption, risk assessment, policies, incident response, and regular audits.
The business objective changes, but the control family often remains similar.
Evidence must show that controls are designed, implemented, operating, and reviewed.
workflows: Identity Management -> Least Privilege -> Authentication -> Logging -> Monitoring -> Encryption -> Risk Assessment -> Policies -> Incident Response -> Regular Audits
oneLiners: Many frameworks share controls but apply them to different risks.

topics.title: Encryption as a Shared Control
topics.definition: Encryption is a common control that supports different compliance goals depending on the protected data type.
keyPoints: Encryption protects financial reports under SOX when those reports or supporting data are sensitive.
Encryption protects cardholder data under PCI DSS.
Encryption protects electronic PHI under HIPAA.
Encryption supports confidentiality and protection of personal data under GDPR.
COBIT recommends governance around selecting, implementing, managing, monitoring, and improving encryption controls.
ISO 27001 can manage encryption through risk assessment, control selection, and operational procedures.
oneLiners: One control can support many obligations when mapped correctly.

topics.title: Data Classification Map
topics.definition: Data classification links the type of information to the framework or management system most relevant to its protection.
keyPoints: Financial data maps to SOX.
Cardholder data maps to PCI DSS.
Protected Health Information maps to HIPAA.
Personal data maps to GDPR.
Business information maps to COBIT governance and ISO 27001 security management.
oneLiners: Classify the data to know which rules matter.

topics.title: CIA Triad Framework Map
topics.definition: The CIA Triad supports all frameworks, but some frameworks emphasize one pillar more strongly.
keyPoints: Confidentiality is strongly visible in HIPAA, GDPR, PCI DSS, and ISO 27001.
Integrity is strongly visible in SOX because financial information must be accurate and complete.
Availability is strongly visible in COBIT and ISO 27001 because business and IT services must remain usable.
PCI DSS uses all three: card data must remain confidential, accurate, and available for secure payment processing.
workflows: CIA Triad -> Confidentiality -> HIPAA + GDPR + PCI DSS + ISO 27001
CIA Triad -> Integrity -> SOX + PCI DSS + ISO 27001
CIA Triad -> Availability -> COBIT + ISO 27001 + PCI DSS
oneLiners: CIA is the security language underneath most compliance frameworks.

topics.title: Compliance Memory Hook
topics.definition: A short memory hook maps each compliance area to a keyword.
keyPoints: Money maps to SOX.
IT maps to COBIT.
Cards map to PCI DSS.
Patients map to HIPAA.
People or personal data maps to GDPR.
Security management maps to ISO 27001.
oneLiners: Money, IT, cards, patients, people, and management.

topics.title: GDPR Philosophy
topics.definition: GDPR treats personal data as belonging to the individual, with organizations acting as custodians rather than owners.
keyPoints: The central protected interest is personal data and privacy.
Organizations may process data only when there is a legitimate and lawful basis.
GDPR gives individuals enforceable rights over their data.
Accountability requires the organization to demonstrate responsible processing.
workflows: Personal Data -> Belongs to Individual -> Organization as Custodian -> Lawful Processing -> Accountability
oneLiners: Under GDPR, organizations hold personal data; they do not truly own it.

topics.title: GDPR Scope
topics.definition: GDPR scope is driven by whose data is processed, not only where the company is located.
keyPoints: An organization outside the European Union may still fall under GDPR if it processes EU residents' data.
The individual location and data-subject relationship are central.
This makes GDPR internationally relevant for digital businesses.
workflows: EU Resident Data -> Processed by Organization Anywhere -> GDPR May Apply
oneLiners: For GDPR, the data subject matters more than the company address.

topics.title: GDPR Protected Information
topics.definition: GDPR personal data is broad and includes direct identifiers, online identifiers, and sensitive categories.
keyPoints: Identity data includes name, address, email, and phone.
Online identity includes IP address, cookies, login identifiers, and geolocation.
Special or sensitive categories may include health, biometrics, genetics, political views, religion, trade union membership, and sexual orientation.
The scope is much broader than name and email.
workflows: Personal Data -> Identity + Online Identity + Health + Biometrics + Genetics + Beliefs + Membership + Sensitive Attributes
oneLiners: GDPR personal data includes any information that can identify or relate to a person.

topics.title: Controller vs Processor
topics.definition: A controller decides why and how personal data is processed. A processor processes data on behalf of the controller.
keyPoints: The controller owns most compliance responsibility because it determines purpose and means.
The processor follows controller instructions and must protect the data it handles.
Example: an online business deciding data collection may be controller; a cloud provider storing the database may be processor.
workflows: Controller -> Decides Why and How -> Processor -> Processes Data for Controller
oneLiners: Controller decides; processor performs.

topics.title: Meaning of Processing
topics.definition: Processing under GDPR is broad and includes almost every interaction with personal data.
keyPoints: Processing includes collecting, recording, storing, modifying, retrieving, sharing, erasing, destroying, combining, and using data.
Storage alone can count as processing.
Deletion and destruction are also processing activities because they affect personal data.
workflows: Collect -> Record -> Store -> Modify -> Retrieve -> Share -> Use -> Erase -> Destroy
oneLiners: If personal data is handled, it is probably processing.

topics.title: Seven GDPR Principles
topics.definition: GDPR is built around seven principles that guide lawful and responsible personal-data processing.
keyPoints: Lawfulness, fairness, and transparency require proper legal basis and clear communication.
Purpose limitation means data should be collected for specific purposes.
Data minimization means only necessary data should be processed.
Accuracy requires personal data to be correct and updated.
Storage limitation limits how long data is kept.
Integrity and confidentiality require security protection.
Accountability requires the organization to demonstrate compliance.
workflows: Lawfulness -> Purpose Limitation -> Data Minimization -> Accuracy -> Storage Limitation -> Integrity and Confidentiality -> Accountability
oneLiners: GDPR principles turn privacy into operational rules.

topics.title: Individual Rights under GDPR
topics.definition: GDPR gives individuals control-oriented rights over their personal data.
keyPoints: Individuals have the right to be informed.
They can access their data.
They can request rectification.
They can request erasure.
They can restrict processing.
They can receive data portability.
They can object to processing.
They receive protections around automated decision-making and human review.
workflows: Know My Data -> See My Data -> Correct My Data -> Delete My Data -> Restrict Use -> Take My Data Elsewhere -> Object -> Human Review
oneLiners: GDPR gives people rights over how their data is handled.

topics.title: GDPR Compliance Workflow
topics.definition: GDPR compliance requires lawful processing, documentation, risk management, training, monitoring, audit, and breach response.
keyPoints: Consent may be one legal basis, but it is not the only one.
Privacy notices or policies communicate processing clearly.
Records of processing activities support accountability.
Risk assessment helps identify privacy and security impact.
Employee training supports correct handling of personal data.
Monitoring and audit verify whether privacy controls work.
Certain breaches require notification within 72 hours.
workflows: Consent or Legal Basis -> Privacy Notice -> Records -> Risk Assessment -> Training -> Monitor -> Audit -> 72-Hour Breach Notification
oneLiners: GDPR compliance is accountability plus rights plus breach readiness.

topics.title: ISO 27001 Philosophy
topics.definition: ISO 27001 treats information security as a managed, risk-driven, continuously improving system.
keyPoints: It is not just a technical checklist.
It defines how the organization manages information security.
Its philosophy is security through management, continuous improvement, and risk reduction.
It uses the ISMS as the central organizing concept.
workflows: Security -> Management -> Continuous Improvement -> Risk Reduction
oneLiners: ISO 27001 manages security as a system.

topics.title: Information Security Management System
topics.definition: An ISMS is the organized set of people, processes, technology, risk activities, policies, controls, monitoring, and improvement used to manage information security.
keyPoints: The ISMS is the core concept of ISO 27001.
It combines people, processes, and technology.
It is driven by risk.
It should improve continuously.
Security becomes an ongoing management process rather than a one-time project.
workflows: ISMS -> People -> Processes -> Technology -> Risk -> Continuous Improvement
oneLiners: ISMS is the management engine of ISO 27001.

topics.title: CIA Triad in ISO 27001
topics.definition: ISO 27001 ultimately protects confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
keyPoints: Confidentiality protects information from unauthorized disclosure.
Integrity protects information from unauthorized or improper modification.
Availability ensures information and systems are accessible when needed.
The CIA Triad helps define security objectives for risk assessment and control selection.
workflows: ISO 27001 -> ISMS -> Controls -> Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability
oneLiners: CIA is the objective; ISMS is the management system.

topics.title: ISO 27000 Family
topics.definition: The ISO 27000 family contains information security management standards, with ISO 27001 and ISO 27002 serving different purposes.
keyPoints: ISO 27000 refers to the broader ISMS family.
ISO 27001 defines requirements.
ISO 27002 provides control implementation guidance.
A useful memory line is: 27001 tells what must exist; 27002 helps explain how controls can be implemented.
workflows: ISO 27000 Family -> ISO 27001 Requirements -> ISO 27002 Control Guidance
oneLiners: 27001 is requirement; 27002 is guidance.

topics.title: ISO Risk-Centric Design
topics.definition: ISO 27001 is risk-centric: assets, threats, vulnerabilities, and business impact drive treatment and control selection.
keyPoints: The organization identifies assets.
It analyzes threats and vulnerabilities.
It assesses risk.
It treats risk.
It selects and implements controls.
It monitors and improves the system.
workflows: Assets -> Threats -> Vulnerabilities -> Risk -> Assessment -> Treatment -> Controls -> Monitoring
oneLiners: ISO controls should be justified by risk.

topics.title: Annex A Controls
topics.definition: Traditional ISO 27001 Annex A organized controls across multiple domains such as policies, people, assets, access, cryptography, operations, suppliers, incidents, continuity, and compliance.
keyPoints: Traditional Annex A included 114 controls organized into 14 domains.

Domains included security policies, HR security, asset management, access control, cryptography, physical security, operations, communications, secure development, suppliers, incidents, business continuity, and compliance.
Annex A helps organizations select controls relevant to identified risk.
oneLiners: Annex A is the control catalogue, not the whole ISMS.

topics.title: ISMS Lifecycle
topics.definition: The ISMS lifecycle moves from management support and scope definition through risk assessment, control implementation, monitoring, internal audit, management review, and certification.
keyPoints: Management support is necessary for authority and resources.
Scope defines what the ISMS covers.
Risk assessment identifies what must be treated.
Risk treatment selects responses and controls.
Statement of Applicability records selected controls and justifications.
Training, monitoring, internal audit, management review, and certification sustain the system.
workflows: Management Support -> Define Scope -> Risk Assessment -> Risk Treatment -> Statement of Applicability -> Implement Controls -> Training -> Monitoring -> Internal Audit -> Management Review -> Certification
oneLiners: The ISMS lifecycle turns risk treatment into managed security.

topics.title: PDCA Cycle
topics.definition: PDCA is the continuous improvement cycle used to keep management systems effective.
keyPoints: Plan defines objectives, risk treatment, controls, and processes.
Do implements planned controls and processes.
Check measures, monitors, audits, and reviews performance.
Act improves the system based on results.
The cycle repeats.
oneLiners: PDCA keeps the ISMS alive.

topics.title: Relationships Between Frameworks
topics.definition: Different regulations protect specific domains, while ISO 27001 provides a security management system that can support many of them.
keyPoints: SOX protects financial reporting.
HIPAA protects health information.
PCI DSS protects card data.
GDPR protects personal data.
ISO 27001 provides a security management system that helps protect information under many obligations.
COBIT provides enterprise IT governance that can organize controls and responsibilities.
workflows: Specific Regulations: SOX + HIPAA + PCI DSS + GDPR
oneLiners: Specific laws define obligations; governance and security management help deliver them.

topics.title: Unified Compliance Hierarchy
topics.definition: A useful hierarchy places COBIT at governance level, ISO 27001 at security management level, and specific regulations below as domain-specific obligations.
keyPoints: Business objectives drive governance.
COBIT supports IT governance.
ISO 27001 supports security management.
Specific regulations and standards define obligations for financial reporting, privacy, healthcare, and payment data.
ISO certification alone does not automatically prove compliance with SOX, GDPR, HIPAA, or PCI DSS.
workflows: Business -> Governance: COBIT -> Security Management: ISO 27001 -> Specific Obligations: SOX + GDPR + HIPAA + PCI DSS
oneLiners: COBIT governs IT, ISO manages security, and regulations define domain obligations.

topics.title: Compliance Knowledge Graph
topics.definition: The long-term compliance map connects governance, financial reporting, payment security, healthcare privacy, personal-data privacy, and information-security management.
keyPoints: Governance connects to COBIT.
Financial compliance connects to SOX.
Payment compliance connects to PCI DSS.
Healthcare compliance connects to HIPAA.
Privacy compliance connects to GDPR.
Security management connects to ISO 27001.
Shared controls create relationships among all frameworks.
oneLiners: A compliance knowledge graph prevents isolated memorization.

topics.title: Final Memory Map
topics.definition: Money maps to SOX, IT maps to COBIT, cards map to PCI DSS, patients map to HIPAA, people map to GDPR, and security management maps to ISO 27001.
keyPoints: SOX: financial reporting and internal controls.
COBIT: IT governance, risk, controls, and maturity.
PCI DSS: cardholder data and payment security.
HIPAA: PHI and patient privacy.
GDPR: personal data and individual rights.
ISO 27001: ISMS, risk treatment, controls, and continuous improvement.
oneLiners: Money, IT, cards, patients, people, and security management form the core compliance map.
